q: Sound is a sequence of pressure waves which propagates through compressible media.
During their propagation, waves can be reflected, refracted or attenuated by the medium.
The speed of sound within a medium is independent of temperature.
Viscosity determines the rate at which sound is attenuated.
In many media, such as air or water, attenuation due to viscosity is extremely high.
The speed of sound will be the same for all different media.
Longitudinal sound waves travel faster in gases than they do in solids.
Young's modulus and the bulk modulus are elasticity properties of the medium.
The SI units of Young's modulus and the bulk modulus are $N \cdot m^{-2}$.
The minimum intensity of sound that can be heard is called the threshold of hearing.
The reference intensity value $I_0$ is taken as $10^{-10} W \cdot m^{-2}$.
The decibel ($dB$) is the unit used for measuring sound intensity.
For every $10 dB$ added to the sound level, the loudness of the sound is increased by 100 times.
Sound intensity is a direct measure of the power reaching an eardrum.
The intensity level of sound $\beta$ is measured relative to a reference intensity $I_0$.
Echoes are produced where a sound wave is reflected from a large and hard surface.
An echo is a repetition of sound due to the reflection of sound.
Reflection of sound violates the laws of reflection.
Rugs and carpets increase the amount of sound reflecting off floor surfaces.
Echolocation is the special ability of emitting sounds and interpreting the echoes.
Bats are known to use echolocation to navigate and locate their prey.
A reverberation is a reflection of light or sound waves.
The sound bouncing around in a large speaker is an example of a reverberation.
Padding and carpets absorb the incident sound and reduce the amount of sound reflecting.
Walls in buildings are covered with padding to increase the effect of echo.
Audible means the ability to be heard or capability of detecting sounds in certain range of frequencies.
For humans with normal hearing, the lower limit of audibility is $20 Hz$.
The upper limit of the range of audibility for humans is about $200,000 Hz$.
Sonar technology works based on the principle of echoes.
Ships use sonar for navigation at sea and to detect the position of other vessels.
Infrasound waves have frequencies higher than $20 Hz$.
Infrasound waves have long wavelengths and can travel over long distances.
Elephants are able to talk using frequencies as low as $15 Hz$.
Infrasound waves travel longer distances with more attenuation than audible sound.
A rhinoceros uses frequencies down to $10 Hz$ to communicate.
Ultrasound refers to frequencies within the human audible range.
The Doppler effect is the change in frequency of a wave related to an observer due to relative motion.
The Doppler effect is named after the Austrian physicist Christian Doppler.
Doppler effect is observed only in sound and not in electromagnetic radiations.
Red shift occurs when the earth and a star are moving away from each other.
In red shift, the wavelength of light from a star shifts towards a higher frequency.
Radar is a form of echolocation that uses electromagnetic waves instead of sound.
Police radar, air traffic control radar and weather radar are applications of the Doppler effect.
Doppler ultrasound is a technique used to examine blood flow.
Angiodynography is a technique to measure the Doppler shift in frequency of ultrasound reflected by white blood cells.
Echocardiography can be used to examine organ such as heart.
The pitch of the note is determined by its frequency.
The greater the frequency, the higher is the pitch.
The loudness of the note depends upon the wavelength that produces it.
The greater the amplitude, the louder the note.
Quality or timbre distinguishes two sounds of different loudness and pitch.
Timbre of a sound depends on its waveform.
The intensity of a wave is directly proportional to the square of the amplitude of the wave.
Loudness is a physical property that does not depend on the observer.
To human ears, the loudness of the sound is subjective.
Normally the greater the intensity, the greater the loudness of the sound is.
Quality of sound varies with the number of overtones or harmonics.
The intensity of sound is the energy transmitted per second per unit area held normally to direction of sound.
